trajectory_summary: The call opened flat, briefly stabilized through the verify section, then collapsed at the first installation-configuration question. Eleanor was engaged enough to propose her own solution (ground-mount) — a genuine enthusiasm signal — and Bucky ended the call by denying the solution rather than bridging to a specialist. The lead was not disqualified by Eleanor's situation; it was disqualified by Bucky's unilateral product-scope determination.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is 'unknown' but the observable outcome is a lost lead — not a DQ. Eleanor is a homeowner, sole title-holder, on a rural property with wide-open sun exposure, a flat roof she has already thought through, and a preference for ground-mount. None of the schema hard DQ rules apply. The call ended because Bucky stated Ion does not do ground-mount installations without bridging to a specialist to confirm. Per coaching_philosophy.setter_scope_discipline, installation configuration is closer-tier content. The correct outcome should have been a tentative appointment or at minimum a held lead pending specialist confirmation — not a terminated call.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Setter scope discipline — escape hatch vs unilateral product denial
primary_coaching_focus.why: This four-word line ended a recoverable call with a qualified, engaged customer. Bucky made a product-scope determination that is not his job to make. The universal escape hatch — 'that's a question for our specialist, let me get you on the calendar' — is the only correct response to any installation-configuration question. Until Bucky has this reflex, he will keep closing doors that should stay open.

key_moments.quote: This is, this is Bucky with Ion. I'm reaching out about the solar for me for the online. How are you doing? Okay. Yeah.
key_moments.stewart_read: The intro fails on three counts: the script is garbled ('solar for me for the online'), Bucky self-answers 'how are you doing' without pausing for Eleanor, and there is no legitimacy anchor — no Tesla framing, no form-fill reference that would tell Eleanor why this call is legitimate. The schema intro beat requires a clear company introduction, a reference to the form submission, and a human pause for response. None of the three land.

key_moments.quote: No. No. No. No. This is wide open sagebrush. There's no issue with solar here.
key_moments.stewart_read: Eleanor volunteers that her rural property has zero shading or obstruction — she has already assessed her own solar suitability and is telling Bucky the site is clean. This is an unsolicited qualification signal. Bucky's response ('No. No. No. I'm not saying that there is') is defensive rather than anchoring on what Eleanor just handed him.

key_moments.quote: Well, there's a home here. Maybe the map is old. I don't know. With your Google Maps doesn't find this.
key_moments.stewart_read: Eleanor is telling Bucky she lives in a rural area where Google Maps doesn't resolve her address — a practical reality that explains the satellite-view gap. This is a trust-building opportunity: acknowledging the rural-property context would have signaled to Eleanor that Bucky understands her situation. Instead Bucky moves on without acknowledgment. The moment passes unused.

key_moments.quote: Oh, well, then I don't qualify because that's under a 100.
key_moments.stewart_read: Eleanor applies her own incorrect qualification threshold and begins to exit the call. Bucky's bare denial ('Oh, no. That doesn't mean you don't qualify') stops the bleed but provides no substance — no explanation of what the actual threshold is, no flip of the bill into emotional alignment, no anchor on what her bill pain actually represents. The schema requires capture AND flip; Bucky delivers neither.
